chapters.title: Working with machines: robots and the possibility of job cuts
chapters.text:
Imagine a few minutes of big city life. A skirmish between football fans did not go unnoticed, as ATM cameras recorded it. A streetcleaner has a GPS transponder that notifies the mayor's office about the route and provides citizens with real-time information. And the tourist, who left a bookstore and went for a walk with a hidden secret, a stolen book in one of his jacket pockets. A program that records the store's inventory will later detect the loss. We don't have privacy even for a minute; cameras, pedometers, cash registers, and other technologies record everything.

At any other time, it would be impossible to capture so many activities taking place at the same time in different parts of the city. But today, machines and programs follow us everywhere. For example, we have fun, find partners and friends, work, and relax with the help of smartphones. The phone accompanies us almost every moment of life and makes it easier. At the same time, constant smartphone usage deprives life of spontaneity and humanity. In 2005, scientists conducted a study by asking people to show the contents of their bags. There were photographs of loved ones, icons, amulets, and money. But in ten years, the cell phone had replaced all these items. On the one hand, this is a logical consequence of technological progress, and on the other, it indicates our helplessness and dependence.

We cannot imagine life without the convenience of technology. But what lies behind the friendly shell of progress? Adam Greenfield wrote the book Radical Technologies to expose technical systems from different angles, not just from the attractive side, as manufacturers do for their benefit.

Our lives depend on technology, but we are not aware of the structure of most of the devices we encounter.

You will explore extraordinary technologies and products and their presence in our lives. You will analyze the work of familiar technologies (smartphones) and reality-changing ones (artificial intelligence) and find out their positive and negative consequences. Learn how radical technologies change us and whether it is possible to maintain independence and humanity in the coming era of machines.

chapters.title: Beware, they hear you: the secret life of voice assistants
chapters.text:
A voice assistant lives in our phones, computers, and tablets, like a genie in a magic lamp. Google, Microsoft, Amazon, and Apple have created assistants that process voice requests and provide the necessary information or connect to the proper application. Predominantly, these assistants have female names and voices; a study in North America showed that users trust women more than they trust men. Apple has Siri, and Amazon has Alexa; only Google created an assistant, aptly named Google, and deprived it of gender identity.

The problem with voice assistants lies in several points:
• The habit of using voice assistants makes us irritated faster when we don't instantly get what we want.
• We rely on technology to deal with our needs too much.
• Assistants listen to us constantly.

For example, Dash Button, an Amazon product, connects to the Web to buy goods with a single touch of a button. We also need the Amazon app and an account for it. The device allows us to make purchases quickly and cancel them if necessary. We get the items, and Amazon gets our addresses and phone numbers, activity, and requests. The company will create our behavioral models and exploit them in the future, sending notifications about sales and targeting ads.

Any device connected to the network is bait, and the market is the fisherman.

What happens behind the scenes of our Amazon purchases? At Amazon's fulfillment center, temperatures rise to critical levels during the summer as the doors are constantly closed to prevent theft. As a result, warehouse workers suffer from cancer and other diseases. Our comfortable shopping experience is just the visible side of the coin.

Home assistants or outdoor devices — our technologies actively and aggressively collect all possible information about our lives. Voice assistants create the illusion of caring, but it is not real. The question of who owns all the data that we carelessly give the Internet remains open. We can be more careful about our data and leave it only in places where we make frequent purchases. We can also limit the use of voice assistants to preserve autonomy.

chapters.title: What we really get when we buy a smartphone
chapters.text:
The invention of smartphones has both simplified and complicated our lives. On the one hand, a compact phone contains a player, a notebook, a camera, and even a telephone booth. One move — and we get access to the required option. On the other hand, our world strives for dematerialization. We are losing essential rituals like sending greeting cards or raising a hand to catch a cab. Now we can send a picture or call a taxi using a single device. Of course, people would not allow so many important rituals to disappear if they did not make life easier. At the same time, we do not notice how a dead smartphone battery or lack of the Internet makes us panic.

The size of smartphone screens increases with the release of each new model. The touch screen is the most attractive part of the phone since we constantly interact with it, scrolling feeds and zooming. The developers rely not only on your convenience but also that a phone with a large screen automatically seems desirable. We require more and more memory from developers to store all the information on the phone.

Although big storage increases the price of a smartphone, we are willing to pay more to keep our entire lives in it.

Thanks to GPS and satellites in orbit, the smartphone always knows where it is. Maps are an outstanding achievement of progress since we can easily find out where we are anywhere in the world. However, the more we rely on smartphones, the more our helplessness grows. The inability to control the situation confuses us and creates a false feeling that the world suddenly became hostile.

Regardless of where the smartphone was developed, there is a possibility that it was assembled in a factory with low wages and a high suicide rate among workers. Smartphones do not work without raw materials, which miners extract in Congo. Mining waste has destroyed 70% of coral reefs and continues to pollute the water.

Reasonable consumption is the right solution to environmental problems. The prospect of buying a new smartphone is attractive but try to resist it if your phone is serviceable. In addition, time away from smartphones helps prevent addiction to social networks.

chapters.title: It is impossible to exist in multiple realities even in the advanced future
chapters.text:
In summer 2016, Shayla Wiggins decided to catch a water monster for her Pokemon Go collection. The app led her to a river where she accidentally discovered the body of a 28-year-old man in the water. We do not know how long the incident would have remained unsolved if augmented reality had not taken the young woman outside.

There is a difference between virtual and augmented reality. The first involves wearing a helmet and headphones that isolate you from the real world with simulated images and sounds. Augmented reality blends its worlds with reality, making it more voluminous and complementing our senses. Augmented reality was initially supposed to be useful, for example, to help people who have difficulty remembering faces. However, this process only complicates communication — try explaining to your friend that you brought the phone to their face to find their profile on social media and remember the name!

Augmented reality is fascinating as entertainment, but it entails the danger of being robbed. When a person moves with the phone extended forward, they become vulnerable and disoriented, and in this state, a thief can easily knock them down. Also, one day, Pokemon Go players came to the 9/11 memorial looking for monsters, which was perceived as an act of disrespect. Thus, the additional reality confirms that it is impossible to exist in two places simultaneously.

Philip K. Dick once defined reality as “that which, when you stop believing in it, doesn't go away,” and it's this bedrock quality of universal accessibility—to anyone at all, at any time of his or her choosing—that constitutes reality's primary virtue. ~ Adam Greenfield

Augmented reality is a mediator between us and the real world, implying that our lives are inferior and need improvement. Where augmented reality was supposed to be an assistant, inconsistencies arise, and sometimes, the information it broadcasts turns out to be incorrect. For example, if you open the walk mode on Google maps, you will see many places already closed and out of date. In addition, the constant use of augmented reality technologies scatters attention and later can lead to more severe disorders. The release of Pokemon Go provoked a surge in injuries and fatalities as players lost their vigilance.

Before you explore virtual reality, make sure you understand the one where you live.

chapters.title: It is okay to fill your wallet with cash, not cryptocurrency
chapters.text:
Satoshi Nakamoto is the pseudonym of the person or group of inventors responsible for developing the Bitcoin cryptocurrency, making him even more mysterious. Nakamoto's identity is still unknown, but a document describing a new digital currency in the Cryptography mailing list in 2008 was the first to mention his name. Experts who have analyzed his writing style found out that British English is probably his first language. But that's all we know about him.

Bitcoin was designed to replace cash and allow users to pay fast and easily. Before the advent of Bitcoin, the danger of dealing with digital currencies was the need to trust an intermediary. Mediators got access to a lot of information, which was unacceptable for those who wanted to maintain confidentiality. Also, some of these transactions occurred in multiples while some did not take place at all.

The innovation of Bitcoin was its architecture, which assigned users unique identifiers promising security in the ownership history of a particular coin. Each action is a digital code, and each subsequent block of activities contains information from the previous block, forming a blockchain. Thus, it is a database and a way to encrypt and transfer data.

But why do we still not pay with bitcoins at every step and do not see bitcoin price tags in stores? Firstly, this currency is extremely unstable, and keeping savings in it becomes a cause of constant nervousness — the rate soared to 60 thousand dollars, then fell to 164 dollars. Secondly, transactions with bitcoins are very slow — small transactions take about ten minutes, and large amounts can take more than one hour.

You cannot pay for tickets for flights on well-known airlines using cryptocurrency. Starting and replenishing a bitcoin wallet is not easy and finding someone who accepts payments in cryptocurrency is even more difficult. And you won't buy chewing gum or water in the nearest store with Bitcoins. Unfortunately, cryptocurrency does not justify the number of resources spent on them. Mining cryptocurrency burns a lot of energy, and “farms” generate problematic amounts of heat. While the benefits of Bitcoins are still blurred, the threat of global warming remains very real.

Cryptocurrency is promising, but you should consider whether you need its benefits now, not in the future.

Did you know? By 2015, the British government stated that the Bitcoin network consumed a daily amount of energy comparable to the electricity use of Ireland.

chapters.title: Working with machines: robots and the possibility of job cuts
chapters.text:
Machines are stronger and faster than humans; they do not experience laziness, boredom, or fatigue. Economist John Maynard Keynes invented the term “technological unemployment” in 1928, indicating that technology would take most human jobs in the future.

The creation of intelligent machines is not only progress, which can make life easier; it is also a real challenge for the human intellect. The invention of a device that can reach the human level of thinking will be one of, if not the main breakthroughs of humankind. Another motivational factor for building such machines is that they won't charge for overtime or complain about working conditions. Progress for bosses looks like getting a reliable and compliant workforce.

Automation is already becoming part of our reality. For example, in 2014, McDonald's began introducing touch screen terminals for orders. The company assured that the machines would not take away jobs but allow employees to work in more “productive” roles. At the same time, the former head of the American division of McDonald's, Ed Rensi, said that it is cheaper for companies to acquire a robot than to raise the wages of inefficient employees. In 2009, the Japanese company Keikyu Corporation began to measure and evaluate the “quality” of smiles of ordinary employees.

This approach looks depressing: the owners of expensive boutiques can start replacing consultants with robots because machines do not have burnouts and bad days. Although the attempt to replace people with robots at boring or hard jobs seems noble, it hides the fact that millions of people who do not have sufficient qualifications or documents for other positions will be left without sources of income.

Hard times are coming for those who have nothing to offer the economy but their muscle, their heart, or their sex. ~ Adam Greenfield

There will hardly be at least one task that a machine cannot perform in the future, which will change the economy, society's organization, and our perception of ourselves. We mostly associate automated systems with something good that will help people, not replace them; we see a future with mixed teams of people and robots. However, our desire to completely trust machines can play a cruel joke.

We should value people, not for their work but the simple fact of their existence.

chapters.title: The art of machines can take place in museums, but not in the souls of people
chapters.text:
Now machines learn more complex things, like recognizing people's identities even as they change their hairstyles and grow facial hair or identifying a cup in broken pieces on the floor.

Attempts to train machines do not always end successfully, and it is too early to talk about unambiguous success. In 2015, Tesla released autopilot cars. The company's founder, Elon Musk, claimed that the vehicle would control itself on the road, fix its speed, and park itself. Musk noted that the Model S data would be sent to the Tesla network, allowing the algorithms to train. However, in May 2016, Joshua Brown died, becoming the first victim of an accident involving autonomous vehicles. His Tesla vehicle failed to make out the outline of a white truck against the pale sky. No matter how quickly the machines learn, it is still too early to give them complete control.

Important inventions sometimes take lives.

Some people think that the concept of inspiration is overrated, and machines can create masterpieces as easily as they can learn color schemes, notes, or storytelling. However, human jazz singers inject the pain and strength of their life experiences into their songs; zeroes and ones cannot express such emotions.

The developers of The Next Rembrandt had their own opinion. They created a 3D-printed Rembrandt portrait with the texture of an oil painting on canvas. The program analyzed 346 authentic Rembrandt paintings and ‘printed’ a new work of art using Rembrandt’s original style and techniques. Technically, it was Rembrandt's iconic work — the experts recreated his typical lines and chiaroscuro (the practice of using a contrast of light and dark to add volume and shadows). But there were no real artist's life experiences, mood, or soul in this image.

When we see technically perfect art, we like it. But when the artist gives their soul to art — we love it.

New talented artists born in the age of digital production risk living unnoticed. Machine-made paintings are difficult to evaluate because they are competent by definition. Their works may be in museums, but such art is not eternal.

We feel delighted with each news about the achievements of technology. The greater the success of artificial intelligence, the greater the human possibilities seem to us. But at the same time, we will feel a constant sense of pressure that machines could replace the talents of humans so quickly.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
We cannot completely hide from technology even if we live in a forest: satellites or electric wires will silently do their work even there. We will choose a coffee shop suggested by an algorithm, and kids will have fun playing an augmented reality game outside. Technology surrounds us, and with all its complexity and daunting implications, it makes our lives exciting and easy.

The more we let technology into our lives, the more we need to know about it. We can change phones, use a robot vacuum cleaner instead of a regular device, and master cryptocurrency. But all these processes require understanding. Learn the risks and policies of the companies whose products and services you use. And always think sensibly: can you do things without a voice assistant? If you are a young mother with many responsibilities, it will become your friend. But do you want your child to prefer to communicate with it and not with human friends in the yard? Approach the issue of technology wisely and implement it only where it is needed.

A new technology offering means you have a choice, not an obligation to buy it.

Blockchains, 3D printers, algorithms — none of these can produce the exact results their inventors intended. But once they become part of reality, they change it. If you want to open a cryptocurrency wallet or buy a new smartphone, you can do it without feeling guilty. But don’t let automation determine all your decisions — remember that every coin has a second side, and someone's losses may turn out to be more global than your gains. We can live in harmony with technology when we do not forget our own capabilities.

Try this

Phone addiction is one of the major problems of our time, especially among teenagers. Constant monitoring of social media increases anxiety, so there are several steps you can take to avoid phone addiction:
• Turn off push notifications in apps.
• Don't use your phone an hour before bed and not until an hour after waking up.
• Set restrictions on social media use.
